Title: The cellulose synthase-like F3 (CslF3) gene mediates cell wall polysaccharide synthesis and affects root growth and differentiation in barley

Common_Genes.Protein_Family_or_Domain: Cellulose synthase-like D family; glycosyltransferase family 2 (GT2)
Common_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Required for normal root hair elongation; csld5 mutants show short/disrupted root hairs, and the phenotype is rescued by AtCslD5 or heterologous HvCslF3 expression
Common_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Mutant phenotype analysis; complementation with pUbq10::AtCslD5 and pUbq10::HvCslF3; root hair length and density quantification
Common_Genes.Interacting_Proteins: AtCslD3; HvCslF3 (functional replacement in complementation assays)
Common_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Seedling roots grown on half-strength MS or agar medium under controlled conditions
Common_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Overexpression complementation increased root hair density; no other major seedling defects reported
Common_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. AtCslD5 is necessary for normal Arabidopsis root hair elongation. 2. The csld5 mutant displays short and disrupted root hairs. 3. AtCslD5-driven complementation restores the mutant phenotype. 4. HvCslF3 can also rescue csld5, suggesting overlapping function in root hair elongation despite likely biochemical differences.
Common_Genes.Other_Important_Info: The biochemical product of AtCslD5 remains unclear in the paper; it is treated here as a core common gene because its role is central to the functional complementation experiments, but it is not explicitly assigned a demonstrated nutrient-metabolite synthesis reaction in this study.

Common_Genes.Protein_Family_or_Domain: Cellulose synthase-like D family; glycosyltransferase family 2 (GT2)
Common_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Supports root hair initiation and development
Common_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Key determinant of Arabidopsis root hair initiation; csld3 mutants show aborted/abnormal root hairs and are rescued by AtCslD3 but not by HvCslF3
Common_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Mutant phenotype analysis; complementation with pUbq10::AtCslD3 and pUbq10::HvCslF3; root hair phenotyping
Common_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Seedling roots grown on agar medium under controlled conditions
Common_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: HvCslF3 expression in csld3 increased root hair cell number but did not restore normal initiation phenotype
Common_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. AtCslD3 is essential for root hair initiation in Arabidopsis. 2. csld3 mutants show aborted root hairs. 3. AtCslD3 complementation restores the phenotype, whereas HvCslF3 does not, indicating that HvCslF3 acts downstream of or separately from the AtCslD3-dependent initiation step. 4. This establishes AtCslD3 as a distinct functional component from HvCslF3 in root hair development.
Common_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Included as a core comparison gene because complementation assays directly test its role relative to HvCslF3. The paper frames AtCslD3 as epistatic to AtCslD5 in root hair development.

Pathway_Genes.Protein_Family_or_Domain: Cellulose synthase-like F family; glycosyltransferase family 2 (GT2)
Pathway_Genes.Metabolic_Step_Position: Terminal polymer synthase / direct polymer formation step
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Synthesizes the cell wall polysaccharide (1,4)-β-linked glucoxylan
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Accumulation_Site: Barley root tip cell walls, especially elongation zone tissues
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Supports root elongation, root hair development, and normal root differentiation/growth
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Promotes accumulation of (1,4)-β-linked glucoxylan and is required for normal barley root elongation, root system size, cortical development, and root hair formation; in Arabidopsis promotes root hair elongation and density and complements csld5 root hair defects
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: qRT-PCR; mRNA in situ hybridization; RNAi knockdown in barley; polysaccharide quantification by cellulase hydrolysis plus HPLC; heterologous overexpression in Arabidopsis; mutant complementation in csld5 and csld3 backgrounds; root phenotyping and microscopy
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Source_Species: Highest in barley root elongation zone; elevated in epidermis and cortex cells; low in meristem and mature zones
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: In barley root elongation zone; in Arabidopsis expressed constitutively from Ubiquitin10 promoter in transgenic lines
Pathway_Genes.Applied_Promoters: 35S constitutive promoter for barley RNAi silencing; Ubiquitin10 constitutive promoter for Arabidopsis overexpression/complementation
Pathway_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Seedling root development under standard growth conditions; root tissues analyzed at 5–7 days post-germination
Pathway_Genes.Rate_Limiting_or_Key_Control_Step: Key control point for barley root (1,4)-β-linked glucoxylan accumulation and root elongation in this tissue context; rate-limiting not formally established
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: RNAi downregulation causes slower root growth, shorter elongation zone, narrower roots, fewer cortical cell layers, reduced root hair density, and reduced overall root system size
Pathway_Genes.Omics_Data: qRT-PCR expression profiling; no public accession reported
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. HvCslF3 transcripts accumulate strongly in barley root elongation-zone epidermis and cortex. 2. RNAi silencing reduces HvCslF3 expression and significantly decreases endogenous (1,4)-β-linked glucoxylan levels in barley root tips. 3. Reduced HvCslF3 causes shorter seminal roots, a much shorter elongation zone, narrower roots, reduced cortical area/layers, and fewer root hairs. 4. Heterologous expression in Arabidopsis increases root hair length and density. 5. HvCslF3 complements the Arabidopsis csld5 root hair mutant but not csld3, indicating partial functional conservation with CslD-mediated elongation pathways.
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Arabidopsis overexpression altered root hair patterning and epidermal cell fate but did not measurably increase detectable (1,4)-β-linked glucoxylan in Arabidopsis seedlings, suggesting species/context dependence or additional cofactors. The paper contrasts HvCslF3 with HvCslF6, but HvCslF6 is background/context rather than an experimentally central gene in Results.
